description: Catalog of identifiers and forms LLMs commonly reach for from other Scheme dialects (Gerbil, Gambit, Racket, R6RS, R7RS, Common Lisp, Clojure, SRFI) that are wrong in Jerboa/Chez. Each entry pairs the hallucinated form with the correct Jerboa equivalent. Machine-readable companion to divergence.md. Load into jerboa_verify to catch known-wrong usages before code ships.

schema.wrong_source: list of dialects where the form is valid
schema.correct: the correct Jerboa identifier or form
schema.imports: imports required for the correct form (empty = in prelude)
schema.severity: error (won't work), warning (works but wrong idiom), aliased (hallucination now accepted as prelude alias)

entries.notes: hash-has-key? is aliased to hash-key? in (jerboa prelude) for Racket-familiarity.
hash-table-set! is aliased to hash-put! in the prelude.
directory-exists? is aliased to file-directory? in the prelude.
eql? is aliased to eqv? in the prelude.
random-integer is aliased to random in the prelude.
read-line is aliased as a wrapper over get-line that also works with no args (reads current-input-port).
force-output is aliased to a flush-output-port wrapper that accepts 0 or 1 args.
string-map in the prelude is the char-level Racket/R7RS version. Chez's own string-map signature differs; prelude wraps to Racket semantics.
No symbol<? in Jerboa/Chez. Compare via symbol->string + string<?.

entries.correct_example: (if (string-contains s "foo") ...)  ;; returns index or #f — truthy if found
entries.notes: AVAILABLE in Jerboa via: (std misc string-more). Jerboa's string-contains (no ?) returns an index or #f, NOT a boolean. Both values are truthy/falsy in conditionals, so `if` works directly.
Generates make-point, point?, point-x, point-y, point-x-set!. For inheritance: (defstruct (circle shape) (radius)).
raise takes a condition value, not a string. Use error to signal with a message and source. Raise works if you pass a condition object, e.g. (raise (make-error)).
No direct Chez equivalent. Probe via eval + catch for undefined-variable.
AVAILABLE in Jerboa via: (jerboa core), (std gambit-compat), (std srfi srfi-19). Use (time-second t) for epoch seconds, (time-nanosecond t) for sub-second part.

AVAILABLE in Jerboa via: (jerboa core), (std gambit-compat), (std misc thread). Chez sleep takes a time-duration object: (make-time 'time-duration NANOSECONDS SECONDS). For fractional secs, use the nanosecond field.
AVAILABLE in Jerboa via: (jerboa core), (std gambit-compat), (std misc thread). Chez has no thread-yield. sleep with zero duration is the closest equivalent (yields to scheduler).
Gerbil's path-expand is 2-arg; Jerboa's path-expand is 1-arg (~ expansion only). Use path-join for composition.
AVAILABLE in Jerboa via: (jerboa core), (std gambit-compat), (std os fd). Gerbil's process model does not map directly. Use run-process from (std misc process).
AVAILABLE in Jerboa via: (jerboa core), (std gambit-compat). Jerboa/Chez has no user-info record. Read $HOME directly.
Chez uses interaction-environment for the top-level dynamic environment.
Chez formats conditions via display-condition; capture with with-output-to-string.
Gerbil's class system is lower-level. Jerboa provides defstruct (define-record-type) and defclass.
AVAILABLE in Jerboa via: (jerboa core), (std gambit-compat). Use string-replace (Jerboa) or re-replace-all from the regex module for regex-based replacement.
No direct equivalent; use pipe operations from (std os fd) or run-process with piped stdin/stdout.
R6RS make-equal-hashtable works in Chez but prelude's make-hash-table is the Jerboa idiom. For eq-hashtables use (make-eq-hashtable) from (chezscheme).
AVAILABLE in Jerboa via: (jerboa core), (std gambit-compat), (std srfi srfi-151). Chez has bitwise-arithmetic-shift and a short alias ash. arithmetic-shift is not bound.
AVAILABLE in Jerboa via: (std pregexp). Jerboa's regex uses a unified API: re to compile, re-search/re-match?/re-find-all/re-replace. In prelude.

entries.correct_example: (hash-ref ht "k" 0)  ;; default is a value, not a thunk
entries.notes: Jerboa's hash-ref default is a value (not a thunk). Use (hash-get ht key) to get #f if missing.
Chez arg order: predicate first, list second. This is the opposite of Gerbil/SRFI/Racket.
Table first, key second. CL's gethash reverses these.
Chez R6RS order: proc, init, then list(s). SRFI-1 fold has init LAST.
String first, delimiter second. Delimiter must be a CHAR, not a string.
Step is the LAST arg, not middle. 1-arg form: (in-range end). 2-arg: (in-range start end). 3-arg: (in-range start end step).
Gerbil's make-rwlock takes a name; Jerboa's takes 0 args.
list-of? RETURNS a predicate of 1 arg. It's a factory, not a direct test.
maybe is a predicate combinator: (maybe pred) → λval. #f is always accepted.
No #hash reader literal in Chez. Use (hash-table) constructor or list->hash-table.
Jerboa .ss files are plain scheme programs. First form is typically (import ...). Do not write #lang.
For dynamic scoping, use make-parameter + parameterize, not a global var.
AVAILABLE in Jerboa via: (jerboa clojure), (jerboa prelude), (std prelude), (std sugar). Square brackets for params ARE supported (same as parens), but the macro is def not defn.
No fn in Jerboa prelude. Use lambda.

entries.wrong_example: (define-record-type point (mk-point x y) point? (x point-x) (y point-y))
entries.notes: R6RS define-record-type works in Chez but is verbose. defstruct is the Jerboa idiom.
User-facing .ss files are programs, not libraries. Internal .sls files use (library ...) but you should not write those.
Both forms work — the reader supports Gerbil-style :std/path as sugar for (std path). The (std ...) form is canonical and preferred.
Chez uses bytevectors (R6RS). For Gambit compat, (import (std gambit-compat)) provides u8vector aliases.
Jerboa uses -> and ->> for threading. chain is not the Jerboa idiom but may be aliased.
Jerboa names its collecting for-comprehension for/collect. Other Racket for/X forms (for/fold, for/or, for/and) do exist.
No for/vector in Jerboa prelude. Collect to list then convert.

entries.correct_example:
(make-hash-table)  ;; for equal-keyed
;; for eqv-keyed in Chez:
(import (chezscheme))
(make-hashtable equal-hash equal?)
entries.notes: Chez supports make-eqv-hashtable as part of R6RS but Jerboa idiom is make-hash-table (equal?).
Jerboa's displayln accepts multiple values to display on one line: (displayln "a" " " "b"). Port arg is NOT the same as Racket's.

entries.correct_example: (match x ((cons a b) ...))  ;; b is rest
entries.notes: Use (list a b ...) for fixed-length, (cons a rest) for head+tail.
AVAILABLE in Jerboa via: (jerboa core), (std gambit-compat). Jerboa's try/catch is the idiomatic form. guard and with-exception-handler (R6RS) also work.
Chez uses the R6RS condition system. condition?, message-condition?, irritants-condition?, etc.
Jerboa's when-let is single-binding. For multiple, nest them or use cond-let pattern.

entries.wrong: (lambda args body)  ;; args as single symbol
entries.correct_example:
(lambda args (length args))  ;; variadic
(lambda (first . rest) body)  ;; one required + rest
entries.notes: Both forms are valid standard Scheme. Mentioning for completeness; LLMs sometimes mix them up.
parameterize works in Chez. Brackets and parens are interchangeable.
This actually works in Chez/R6RS. Included because LLMs sometimes second-guess whether it exists.

entries.correct_example:
(def t (make-hash-table))
(hash-put! t k v)
(hash-ref t k)
entries.notes: Gambit calls hash tables 'tables'. Use the hash-* family in Jerboa.
Argument order: proc first, table second — opposite of SRFI-69.
AVAILABLE in Jerboa via: (std srfi srfi-125).
hash-get returns #f if missing (like Clojure's get with no default). hash-ref raises.
Jerboa hash tables are mutable. Clojure's functional assoc returns a new map; no direct equivalent without copy.

entries.correct_example:
(length lst)  ;; for lists
(vector-length v) ;; vectors
(string-length s) ;; strings
(hash-table-size ht) ;; hash tables
entries.notes: AVAILABLE in Jerboa via: (jerboa clojure), (std clojure), (std srfi srfi-1).
Classic Lisp car/cdr. first/second/third exist in SRFI-1 (std misc list) but car/cdr is preferred.
Note dotted pair syntax for alists.
Scheme distinguishes #f (false) from '() (empty list). There is no unified nil.
let doesn't destructure in Scheme. Use match or explicit car/cadr.

entries.correct_example: ; .ss programs don't export. For a library, edit the .sls in lib/.
entries.notes: User .ss files are programs. Libraries live in .sls files (internal).
R6RS-style only/except/rename/prefix all work in Chez.
Chez's pp takes 1 arg. Use (pp-to-string form) for string form.
(newline) works; displayln is the Jerboa idiom and accepts multiple values.

entries.wrong_example: (null? "")  ;; #f, not the emptiness test for strings
entries.notes: null? tests for the empty list only. Use type-specific predicates.
Jerboa defmethod uses typed-arg form. Call with {method obj args} or (~ obj 'method args).

entries.correct_example:
(point-x p)  ;; generated by defstruct
;; or using dot-access:
(using (p point : point?) p.x)

entries.notes: No quasiquote unquoting in body. LLMs occasionally emit comma.
## is Gambit's namespace for unsafe/internal ops. Chez uses fx+/fx-/fx* via (chezscheme) for fixnum specifics.

entries.correct_example:
(bytevector-copy bv)  ;; full copy
;; for range:
(define sub (make-bytevector (- end start)))
(bytevector-copy! bv start sub 0 (- end start))
entries.notes: R6RS bytevector-copy is 1-arg. For subrange, use the 5-arg bytevector-copy! with a fresh destination.

entries.correct_example: (get-string-n port 100)  ;; R6RS: port first, count second
entries.notes: Chez/R6RS uses get-string-n with port first.
Without fill arg, initial contents are unspecified in standard Scheme. Always provide a fill if you care.

entries.correct_example: (: value number?)  ;; checked cast, raises if pred fails
entries.notes: Jerboa's : is a runtime checked cast with a predicate, not a type annotation.
Jerboa match uses => for view patterns instead of app.
Check (std misc thread) for current lock primitives.

entries.correct_example:
(import (std concur))
;; check std concur for current channel API
entries.notes: Jerboa has (std concur), (std csp), (std actor) — pick based on your model.

entries.correct_example:
(import (std logger))
;; check (std logger) for current API

entries.notes: Chez format uses Common Lisp directives: ~a, ~s, ~d, ~x, ~%, ~~. No ~v.
Chez uses ~% for newline. ~n is Racket/Gerbil.

entries.correct_example: (bitwise-and #xff x)  ;; Chez uses #x not 0x for hex
entries.notes: Functions exist. Watch for #x vs 0x hex literal syntax (Chez uses #x).
Scheme hex literals use #x prefix. Also #b for binary, #o for octal.
Jerboa uses with-output-to-string / with-input-from-string from the prelude.
No string interpolation. Use the str auto-coerce helper or format.
Note arg order is (string-prefix? PREFIX STRING). Opposite of some Racket naming.
No string-index in prelude. For a single char, use string-contains with 1-char string.

entries.wrong: (str a b c)  ;; Clojure stringifies each arg
entries.notes: Jerboa's str auto-coerces non-strings (same as Clojure). This is a correct usage.
Chez uses positional radix arg (2/8/10/16).
format RETURNS a string. Wrap in displayln/display/printf to output.
R6RS custom ports work in Chez. Included as reassurance.
